Dr. Massimiliano Maule, Prof. Davide Brunelli Collaborative partner Huawei Munich Research Center Date 21 June 2025 Number of pages 67 Language English Abstract This thesis explores the integration of Agentic Artificial Intelligence (Agentic-AI) frameworks with Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to advance the development of intelligent, embodied robotic systems.

modular agent cooperation, indicating that combining VLA models with agentic frameworks can improve flexibility and responsiveness in robotic task execution. Keywords Embodied AI, Robotics, VLAs, AI Agents, 6G

proposes the AgenticVLA system, which combines a pretrained and finetuned VLA model with an open-source agent-based framework. The system is deployed on the Mercury X1 humanoid robot and is capable of both natural language dialogue and environment-aware task execution. A custom Virtual Reality (VR) teleoperation platform was developed to collect a high-quality dual-ar

1 Introduction In cognitive science and philosophy of mind, embodied cognition highlights the critical role of the body’s interaction with its environment in shaping thought processes. This idea extends to Artificial Intelligence (AI) embodiment, which in

ls integrate visual perception, language understanding, and action generation to enable robots to perform tasks in diverse, real-world environments. Notable contributions include models like Octo [2], OpenVLA [3], 𝜋0 [4], and Magma [5], which demonstrate the potential of VLA models in training embodied AI to adapt to unseen scenarios. Despite their promise, cur

een scenarios. Despite their promise, current VLA systems face various limitations. First of all, only a limited number of open-source datasets are specifically designed for robotics manipulation purposes (i.e., Open X-Embodiment [6]). In these datasets, most of the trajectories were performed using single-arm robots, making it almost impossible to just take this model and d

interacting with the environment in multiple ways, such as speaking with a human or picking up an object. This could lead to better autonomous systems, a step towards achieving AGI. 1.1 Research Questions RQ1: Can an Agentic AI Framework be combined with Visual-Language-ActionModel towards achieving AGI? This question examines whether mixing an Agentic AI Framework with VLA mod

prove reasoning capabilities, multimodal perception, and adaptive action, thereby achieving a higher level of intelligence. RQ2: Can a network of AI Agents improve the perception and decision-making capabilities of autonomous robots in a collaborative task setting? If so, how? This question investigates whether a network of AI agents can enhance the perceptual accuracy and decision-makin

utomation, and human-AI interaction. 1.3 Thesis Structure This thesis is organized to follow the chronological development of the project over a six-month period. After the background chapter, which provides the theoretical foundation necessary to understand the approach and results, the methodology chapter outlines the steps taken to set up the experimental environm

results, the methodology chapter outlines the steps taken to set up the experimental environment. The project is structured into four main phases: 1. Teleoperation: A custom teleoperation system was developed to control the Mercury X1 [10] humanoid robot using the Meta Quest 3 headset [11]. This setup enabled immersive, real-time control of the robot for data generation

s structured into four main phases: 1. Teleoperation: A custom teleoperation system was developed to control the Mercury X1 [10] humanoid robot using the Meta Quest 3 headset [11]. This setup enabled immersive, real-time control of the robot for data generation purposes. 2. Data collection: In this phase, a balanced dataset of robotic trajectories was collected using th

ry testing in real-world scenarios to assess performance. 4. Agentic AI integration: Finally, the VLA model was integrated into an Agentic AI framework, combining it with the AutoGen [13] architecture. This phase focused on studying the behavior of the resulting system, referred to as the AgenticVLA, by analyzing its ability to reason, adapt, and interact more auto

del, the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT model and the proposed AgenticVLA system. These experiments examine how each system responds to variations in task phrasing and context, using metrics such as Success Rate (SR) to quantify improvements in reasoning, adaptability, and overall task performance.

ecture. Source: [3] The architecture of OpenVLA, as it is possible to observe from Figure 1, builds on the standard design of recent VLMs, which generally consist of three parts: (1) a visual encoder that processes image inputs into image patch embeddings, (2) a projector that maps these visual embeddings into the input space of a language model, and (3) a LLM backbone. D

odels, aligning well with the objectives of this thesis in studying scalable, efficient, and generalizable robot control policies. 2.1.1 OpenVLA OpenVLA [3] is a 7B-parameter open-source VLA that pushes the boundaries of generalist robot manipulation. It is built on a pretrained, visually-conditioned language model backbone that captures visual features at var

ed, visually-conditioned language model backbone that captures visual features at various granularities, and it has been finetuned on a large and diverse dataset of 970k robot manipulation trajectories from the Open X-Embodiment dataset. One of the key contributions of OpenVLA is its finetuning strategy, which has not been extensively explored in previous work.

key contributions of OpenVLA is its finetuning strategy, which has not been extensively explored in previous work. The model outperforms even larger models, such as the 55B-parameter RT-2-X, and shows significant improvement over other finetuned policies like Octo, as well as from-scratch imitation learning using diffusion policies. In particular, OpenVLA excels at tasks that i

from various online sources. OpenVLA specifically extends the Prismatic-7B VLM, which uses a 600M-parameter visual encoder, a two-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) projector, and a 7B-parameter Llama 2 language model backbone. The visual encoder is a two-part system, combining pretrained Sig

Layer Perceptron (MLP) projector, and a 7B-parameter Llama 2 language model backbone. The visual encoder is a two-part system, combining pretrained SigLIP and DinoV2 models. This combination of visual features helps improve spatial reasoning, which is critical for tasks like robot control. For training OpenVLA, the action prediction problem is framed as a vision-l

s. The continuous robot actions are discretized into discrete tokens, which the language model can predict. Each action dimension is quantized into one of 256 bins, with bin widths based on the quantiles of the training data rather than the min-max range, which helps avoid issues with outlier actions that could affect discretization quality. Finally, the resolu

solution images, while providing more tokens and longer context lengths, also demand more computational power, with little performance gain when compared to using 224x224px images, even though the latter is more efficient during training. 2.1.2 OpenVLA-OFT OpenVLA-OFT [12] is an improved version of OpenVLA, developed by the same research team, that focuses on impro

that focuses on improving the finetuning and inference performaces of the VLA (see Figure 2). They replaced the autoregressive decoding technique from the base model to a parallel decoding approach, which leverages bidirectional attention and generates all actions in a single forward pass. This approach naturally extends to action chunking, predicting the next K actions at

nal attention and generates all actions in a single forward pass. This approach naturally extends to action chunking, predicting the next K actions at each timestep, achieving 43x faster throughput than base OpenVLA. Additionally, OpenVLA-OFT is capable of processing multiple camera views and low-dimensional robot state inputs, such as joint angles and gripper

the available information. This architecture can efficiently digest multimodal inputs and work with dual-arms robots, as the researchers show successful experiments conducted with the ALOHA robot [20]. 2.2 Agentic AI An AI Agent is an autonomous system capable of interacting with the environment, take decisions and execute actions to achieve a specific goal [7].

it to interact with the real world, retrieve up-to-date information, or carry out complex computations that the model alone cannot handle. 3. Instructions: Instructions are the explicit rules, objectives, and behavioral guidelines that shape how the agent operates. This includes its role, tone, permissible actions, and safety constra

ions using multi-agent conversations [13] AutoGen [13] is an open-source framework designed to help developers to build AI Agents and applications, developed by Microsoft. Its generic design, leveraging LLMs, human inputs or tools, enables developers to create agents with different roles and skills, by selecting a subset of already built-in functions or creating cust

loping agentic AI is critical to ensure scalability, completeness and alignment with intended objectives. Currently, the most popular frameworks are AutoGen, LangChain [22], CrewAI [23] or SmolAgents [24], each one with different characteristics and specifics. Agent design foundation In its most fundamental form, an agent consists of three core components (see Figure

tial for exploring embodied intelligence and human-robot interaction. For the purposes of this research, the Mercury X1 wheeled humanoid robot, developed by Elephant Robotics [25], was selected as the primary experimental platform. This chapter provides a detailed introduction to the Mercury X1, focusing on its design philosophy, technical specifications, and relevance to th

igh-performance mobile base with a dual-arm humanoid structure, offering a versatile hardware platform for both research and practical deployment. Elephant Robotics, based in Shenzhen, China, engineered the Mercury X1 to cater to a wide range of users, including researchers, educators, and developers, with an emphasis on flexibility, precision, and ease of integration int

e: [26] The Mercury X1 is a wheeled humanoid robot designed to bridge the gap between theoretical artificial intelligence and real-world robotic applications. Standing at 1.18 meters tall, it combines a high-performance mobile base with a dual-arm humanoid structure, offering a versatile hardware platform for both research and practical deployment. Elephant Robo

2.3.1 Design and Structure The Mercury X1 is composed of two primary components, shown in Figure 5: the Mercury B1 dual-arm semi-humanoid robot and a robust wheeled mobile base. This hybrid design enables the robot to perform tasks requiring both mobility and manipulation, distinguishing it from traditional stationary robotic arms

mobility and manipulation, distinguishing it from traditional stationary robotic arms or purely mobile platforms. The robot features a total of 19 degrees of freedom (DOF), with each of its two seven-axis arms contributing significantly to its dexterity. The arms are constructed with a lightweight carbon fiber shell, which reduces the overall weight to 55 kg

arms contributing significantly to its dexterity. The arms are constructed with a lightweight carbon fiber shell, which reduces the overall weight to 55 kg while maintaining structural rigidity and durability. le maintaining structural rigidity and durability. This design choice enhances the robot’s power efficiency and payload capacity, rated at a maximum of 1 kg. This system operates at up to 90Hz with smooth visual feedback

open-source framework that utilizes the Meta Quest 3 VR headset to control various robots, including mobile manipulators, multifingered hands, and bimanual arms. This system operates at up to 90Hz with smooth visual feedback, allowing operators to perform tasks ranging from tabletop manipulation to contact-rich dexterous operations across both simulated and real

tion to bridge the autonomy gap. 2.4.2 ROS# Figure 6: ROS# Pipeline. Source: [14] ROS# is a set of open-source software libraries and tools in C# for communicating with ROS from .NET applications, in particular Unity (see Figure 6). With ROS#, developers can effortlessly create .NET applications that communicate with ROS nodes, subscribe to and publish topics, handle actions and services, and interact

mera streams, immersive human-inthe-loop control, and collection of demonstration data for training AI models. Unity was selected for this project due to the following key capabilities: • High-quality rendering: Unity’s rendering engine allows for photorealistic visualization of the robot and its environment, improving operator immersion and environmental awareness. • Native VR s

ion platform creates a hybrid reality interface where AI models can be trained and evaluated in a human-centric manner. 2.5 The role of 6G powered systems In June 2023, the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) [41] finalized the framework for the 6G Vision, addressing the fundamental question, "What is 6G?" from two key perspectives [42]. The first focuses on

the 6G Vision, addressing the fundamental question, "What is 6G?" from two key perspectives [42]. The first focuses on the evolution of mobile communications, emphasizing Ultra-Reliable and Low-Latency Communications (URLLC) to enable and support large-scale connectivity. The second perspective extends beyond traditional mobile communications, aiming to achieve Integrated Sensing and Communication (IS

d Low-Latency Communications (URLLC) to enable and support large-scale connectivity. The second perspective extends beyond traditional mobile communications, aiming to achieve Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) while leveraging AI and networking. Figure 7: Connected Intelligence = AGI for 6G + 6G for AGI. Source: [42] A central aspect of 6G is its ability to support large-scale AI-dri

xtends beyond traditional mobile communications, aiming to achieve Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) while leveraging AI and networking. Figure 7: Connected Intelligence = AGI for 6G + 6G for AGI. Source: [42] A central aspect of 6G is its ability to support large-scale AI-driven services and applications. In the era of AI, the core components of applications and servi

bler of real-time, distributed intelligence for AI-powered agents [44, 45]. Major standardization bodies such as the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) and 3GPP have identified robotics as a key vertical for 6G, emphasizing URLLC, ISAC, and semantic-aware data exchange as foundational enablers for collaborative robotic systems [45, 46]. onse, and industrial inspection. Such capabilities demand low-latency, high-throughput networks, which exceed the capacity of current 5G systems in many practical settings. the design, development, and evaluation of an intelligent embodied system based on the Mercury X1 humanoid robot. The overall approach follows a bottom-up strategy, beginning with low-level control and data acquisition, moving through model training, and culminating in high-level system integration within an agentic architecture. nfiguration The teleoperation system is based on the Mercury X1 humanoid robot, equipped with dual seven-axis arms and multiple onboard cameras. The Meta Quest 3 headset and its hand controllers were used to enable immersive control. pped with dual seven-axis arms and multiple onboard cameras. The Meta Quest 3 headset and its hand controllers were used to enable immersive control. The main action performed by the robot in each episode is the pick and place task, where the robot interacts with objects visible in front of it, using one of its two arms to pick up an object and place it on a designated area marked by a plate. each episode, which corresponds to one completed task, was stored in a folder directly on the Meta Quest 3 memory system. The data follows the Reinforcement Learning Dataset (RLDS) [55] format, where every episode consists of a series of steps sampled at a precise frequency during recording. This format is widely used to represent robotic manipulation trajectories and is the same one em

d by the community with custom scripts to convert data into other formats, such as the Lerobot dataset, a flexible and interesting framework developed by HuggingFace [56]. Each episode’s folder contains three subfolders with frames recorded from the three cameras involved, along with a JSON file containing metadata and information about end-effector positions, states,

positions, states, and boolean flags indicating the start and end of the episode. The JSON file follows the structure shown in Table 6. Each image is encoded in PNG format using the RGB color scheme and has a resolution of 640x480 pixels. In the metadata of each episode, the high level task field contains the text instruction related to the task, while the suc

4-dimensional joint states array, a 10-dimensional action array, and boolean values marking the start and end of the episode. The action vector is 10-dimensional because rotation information for each end effector was not recorded, as it was fixed to zero. This decision was made to ensure smoother robot control and reduce data complexity. Instead, what is stored are the end-effe

the robot’s wheeled base. Importantly, the end-effector positions are expressed in the robot base reference frame, meaning that the origin (0,0,0) corresponds to the position of the robot’s wheels. 34

er of finding the most fitting hyperparameters to correctly finetune the VLA model. experiment to reduce modality complexity. Image augmentation was applied to improve visual generalization. er architecture. Using the collected dataset and a modified training configuration, the peak VRAM usage during finetuning reached approximately 62 GB. This is consistent with the expected resource footprint for large-scale VLA models trained on rich episodic data. Following training, the deployment of the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT model was executed on a consumer-gra

els trained on rich episodic data. Following training, the deployment of the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT model was executed on a consumer-grade GPU with 24 GB of VRAM. ed to manage tool-augmented LLM agents using structured, promptbased coordination. At the center of this architecture is a main agent built on LLaMA 3.2 7B [57], which serves as the core reasoning unit and has been chosen considering its efficiency and performances. This agent does not operate in isolation, but instead selectively activates specialized tools based on th

instead selectively activates specialized tools based on the user’s input. One of the central ideas in this implementation is to treat the VLA as a callable function. It is invoked by the main agent only when required, specifically, when the user requests a task that involves perception of the environment or interaction with it. This makes the system modular and scalable wh

sistant but also as a controller capable of interacting with the real world. This hybrid system, combining language, vision, and action, is what we refer to as AgenticVLA (see Figure 13), a VLA model extended with agentic coordination logic and equipped with supporting reasoning and awareness capabilities. Figure 13: AgenticVLA complete framework AutoGen handles input

a user instruction. The following sections describe the tools and logic involved. 3.4.1 Conversational Agent The conversational agent is used whenever the system detects no action-oriented intent in the input. This means that the user is likely asking a question, seeking 38

clarification, or issuing a command that does not involve any interaction with the robot’s environment. The tool uses LLaMA 3.2 7B as its backbone to interpret and respond to these types of inputs. It acts as the default fallback for general interaction, allowing the agent to operate like any LLM-based assistant. The Dialogue Tool ensures that th

3.4.2 Act Agent This agent is responsible for handling all instructions that imply interaction with the physical environment. When the system detects intent through verbs such as “pick up,” “move,” or “put,” this agent is triggered. Rather than directly executing a command, the Act agent first processes the input through two sub-modules: the Reasoning Tool and the

tent through verbs such as “pick up,” “move,” or “put,” this agent is triggered. Rather than directly executing a command, the Act agent first processes the input through two sub-modules: the Reasoning Tool and the Self-Awareness Tool. These modules allow the system to validate the instruction, clarify user intent, and ensure the feasibility of the requested action. After

Reasoning Tool The Reasoning Tool addresses a key limitation of current VLAs: their restricted ability to generalize from natural language. Since many VLAs must be trained on highly structured commands, they can fail when given ambiguous, compound, or loosely phrased instructions. This tool operates as an interprete

LLaMA 3.2 7B is used here to perform the reasoning and rephrasing step, ensuring alignment between human language and the robot’s capabilities. System prompt You are a reasoning layer. Your role is to translate user instructions int

nes: 1. Decompose complex commands into smaller, sequential steps that reflect the capabilities of the robot. 2. Each final task must be formatted as: put the [color] [object] on the [color] plate where [color] and [object] are specific descriptors present in the scene. 3. Verify availability: • If any referenced object or plate color is not confirmed to be present in the scene, do not guess. • I

ction is feasible. This component uses visual input from a third-person camera to generate a description of the current scene and monitor the execution of tasks over time. ments conducted are introduced and discussed. Each experiment is structured as an A vs B vs C evaluation (see Figure 14), comparing three models: • OpenVLA: Serving as the initial experiment to develop a functioning VLA for the Mercury X1 robot, OpenVLA was trained from scratch by combining the OpenX-Embodiment dataset with the custom Mercury X1 dataset. This train

odel to the specific characteristics of the new robotic setup. • Finetuned OpenVLA-OFT: Obtained by finetuning the OpenVLA model on the Mercury X1 dataset using LoRA, this version is more specialized for the specific robot and tasks. • AgenticVLA: Building upon the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT, AgenticVLA integrates the model into the Agentic AI framework introduced ea

uate model performance. The comparison is performed by plotting, over time, the coordinates for each arm and the gripper values, as well as analyzing the Success Rate (SR) and the average Mean Squared Error (MSE). The SR is defined as the ratio between the number of successfully completed tasks, where the robot was able to complete the intended action, and the total num

the gripper values, as well as analyzing the Success Rate (SR) and the average Mean Squared Error (MSE). The SR is defined as the ratio between the number of successfully completed tasks, where the robot was able to complete the intended action, and the total number of tasks performed. The average MSE, instead, measured in millimeters, is calculated by averaging the MSE across the rel

cessfully completed tasks, where the robot was able to complete the intended action, and the total number of tasks performed. The average MSE, instead, measured in millimeters, is calculated by averaging the MSE across the relevant dimensions (x, y, z, grasp) for each arm. rimental pipeline was designed with a strong emphasis on consistency and methodological rigor. A custom-built teleoperation system based on the Meta Quest 3 enabled immersive, high-frequency, and repeatable data acquisition. The use of a standardized RLDS data format ensured compatibility with modern VLA training pipelines and supported a structured and efficient data f

built teleoperation system based on the Meta Quest 3 enabled immersive, high-frequency, and repeatable data acquisition. The use of a standardized RLDS data format ensured compatibility with modern VLA training pipelines and supported a structured and efficient data flow. The data collection process was carefully controlled. Task instructions were predefined, se

t positioning, and sensor configuration. This level of control helped minimize confusing factors and enabled a focused evaluation of system performance. However, the controlled nature of the environment also introduces natural limitations. The results presented may not directly generalize to different robot embodiments, unseen physical layouts, or more complex task domains without further adap

set, these models did not achieve satisfactory performance in the proposed experimental setting. Several factors may help explain this outcome: • Mismatch between architecture and setup: Although OpenVLA-OFT supports multi-view inputs and dual-arm setups, the specific configuration of the Mercury X1 robot, combined with two camera perspectives and language-based task definitions, may have present

pulation tasks that require precise spatial reasoning. Despite these limitations, including OpenVLA in the evaluation was important for two key reasons: • It served as a baseline: Comparing the AgenticVLA system against OpenVLA highlighted the performance improvements enabled by the agentic architecture. This provided a concrete measure of how additional reasoning a

ce of OpenVLA reaffirmed the importance of integrating reactive models within broader control frameworks. VLA systems alone, while powerful, benefit substantially from structured decision-making processes to operate reliably in complex or novel environments. In summary, the OpenVLA model played a valuable role in this work, not as a definitive solution, but as a point of c

lowing responses to the research questions: • RQ1: Can an Agentic AI framework be combined with a Visual-LanguageAction model towards achieving AGI? While this work does not claim to advance toward AGI in any general sense, the AgenticVLA system shows that embedding a VLA model within an agentic control framework increases system flexibility, contextual awareness, and dec

ration is both feasible and beneficial, especially in cases where VLAs on their own are insufficiently robust. However, this approach still requires further development in memory, long-horizon planning, and self-adaptation to be considered a substantial step toward general intelligence. • RQ2: Can a network of AI agents improve the perception and decisionmaking ca

implemented a modular agentic system composed of multiple coordinated agents, each responsible for a distinct function such as reasoning, tool selection, and task execution. While this does not constitute a fully distributed multi-agent system across devices or embodiments, the architecture already demonstrates the value of internal agent specialization and coordination. The u

titute a fully distributed multi-agent system across devices or embodiments, the architecture already demonstrates the value of internal agent specialization and coordination. The use of AutoGen enabled structured communication between agents and facilitated clear separation of roles. The results suggest that structuring control through modular, interacting agents

Future work should extend this architecture toward more decentralized settings, investigating inter-agent communication across physical systems, collaborative task arbitration, and dynamic resource allocation in networked environments. 5.3 Modularity and the Role of Specialized Agents One of the main contributions of this work is the de

and Future Research Directions The work presented in this thesis raises several important questions for future research: • How can multi-agent systems coordinate specialized VLAs for collaborative execution? What mechanisms are required to ensure coherence, prevent redundancy, and resolve conflicts among distributed modules? • To what extent can agentic frameworks generaliz

Challenges and Future Research Directions The work presented in this thesis raises several important questions for future research: • How can multi-agent systems coordinate specialized VLAs for collaborative execution? What mechanisms are required to ensure coherence, prevent redundancy, and resolve conflicts among distributed modules? • To what extent can agentic frameworks generaliz

etability becomes increasingly important. • How can agentic architectures incorporate long-term memory, state persistence, or experience replay? Current implementations operate in an essentially stateless manner, limiting performance in multi-step or multi-session scenarios. 5.5 Final Remarks This thesis has shown that Agentic AI frameworks can partially compensate for the limit

lly stateless manner, limiting performance in multi-step or multi-session scenarios. 5.5 Final Remarks This thesis has shown that Agentic AI frameworks can partially compensate for the limitations of reactive VLA models, particularly by improving instruction interpretation and decision control. The proposed AgenticVLA architecture represents a step toward more modular, context-aware robotic
